channel.title: Nature
channel.description: Nature is the foremost international weekly scientific journal in the world and is the flagship journal for Nature Portfolio. It publishes the finest peer-reviewed research in all fields of science and technology on the basis of its originality, importance, interdisciplinary interest, timeliness, accessibility, elegance and surprising conclusions. Nature publishes landmark papers, award winning news, leading comment and expert opinion on important, topical scientific news and events that enable readers to share the latest discoveries in science and evolve the discussion amongst the global scientific community.
channel.dc:rights: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.
channel.prism:copyright: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.

item.title: High-rise transistors can be used to build space-saving circuits
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 05 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00249-w

Logical circuits have been built from nanosheet stacks of various transistors, which could make electronic devices faster and more compact.
item.dc:title: High-rise transistors can be used to build space-saving circuits
item.dc:creator: Xiong Xiong
Yanqing Wu

item.title: Bulk hexagonal diamond
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 04 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10212-4

The synthesis of millimetre-sized phase-pure hexagonal diamond, a polymorph of cubic diamond, by compressing highly oriented pyrolytic graphite under high pressures and temperatures is reported, providing new insight into the graphite-to-diamond transformation pathway.
item.dc:title: Bulk hexagonal diamond
item.dc:creator: Shoulong Lai
Xigui Yang
Jiuyang Shi
Shijie Liu
Ying Guo
Longbin Yan
Jinhao Zang
Zhuangfei Zhang
Qiuhan Jia
Jian Sun
Shaobo Cheng
Chongxin Shan

item.title: Fresh claim of making elusive ‘hexagonal’ diamond is the strongest yet
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 04 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00711-9

After decades of debate, researchers say that they have found the clearest evidence yet for this rare form of carbon.
item.dc:title: Fresh claim of making elusive ‘hexagonal’ diamond is the strongest yet
item.dc:creator: Mark Peplow

item.title: The squeal of peeling tape, explained
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 02 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00516-w

Weak shock waves triggered by micro-cracks in the adhesive layer of tape cause it to screech when being unspooled from its roll.
item.dc:title: The squeal of peeling tape, explained
